description: The currently provided image is an unrelated software interface button marked with "Check for updates", which is irrelevant to the target research paper quantifying multi-stage recrystallization in low-alloy steel under varied deformation conditions. No valid material science related information, including microstructure characterization results, mechanical property data, simulation outputs, or experimental conclusions of the target research, can be extracted from this irrelevant image. The actual figure content corresponding to the low-alloy steel recrystallization study is completely unreadable in the currently provided display.

This figure illustrates the hot compression experimental procedure and conditions for low-alloy steel for automobile hub bearing production, designed to support dynamic recrystallization research under complex thermomechanical conditions. Subplot (a) presents the full experimental workflow: cylindrical specimens (φ6 mm × 9 mm) are pre-tightened, heated to 1250 °C at 10 °C/s and held for 180 s for austenite homogenization, cooled to target deformation temperature at 10 °C/s and held for 60 s, compressed to a true strain of 0.9, then immediately water quenched to freeze high-temperature microstructures, followed by side-section characterization via optical microscopy (OLYMPUS GX71) and EBSD (SU5000). Constant deformation conditions cover 850 °C to 1150 °C with strain rates 0.01 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹, while varying deformation conditions are set at 950 °C to 1050 °C with strain rates 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹. Subplot (b) shows transient strain rate variation test schemes at 950 °C: samples are first deformed to intermediate true strain εI = 0.2 or 0.4 at initial strain rate 0.01 or 0.1 s⁻¹, then the strain rate is transiently adjusted to 0.1 or 0.01 s⁻¹ until total true strain reaches 0.9. Subplot (c) shows varying temperature test schemes at constant 0.01 s⁻¹ strain rate: samples are first deformed to εI = 0.2 or 0.4 at initial temperature 950 °C or 1050 °C, then temperature is adjusted at 10 °C/s between 950 °C and 1050 °C within strain ranges 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5 until total true strain of 0.9. This standardized experimental design provides controlled data for calibrating and validating the multi-stage recrystallization prediction model developed in the work.

This figure is a schematic of true stress (unit: MPa) versus true strain curves illustrating flow stress evolution of low-alloy steel under varying hot deformation conditions, where Zener-Hollomon parameter Zp (with Zp2>Zp1) represents the combined effect of deformation temperature and strain rate. Four curves are included: black dashed line for constant Zp1, purple dashed line for constant Zp2, blue solid line for Zp decreasing from Zp2 to Zp1 at intermediate true strain εl, and red solid line for Zp increasing from Zp1 to Zp2 at εl. The process is divided into two stages: Stage I (before deformation condition variation, up to εl) and Stage II (after variation). Critical strains for dynamic recrystallization follow the order εc1 < εc2 < εl, and higher Zp corresponds to higher peak stress and steady-state stress. Transient Zp adjustment at εl induces an abrupt stress jump, followed by stress evolution to the steady-state level matching the new Zp condition. This schematic demonstrates the flow stress response under varying thermomechanical conditions, supporting investigation of multi-stage dynamic recrystallization behavior of low-alloy steel during hot forming.

This 3D color-coded surface plot presents the variation of the stress softening coefficient $k_s$ (color scale range: 0.2 to 1, higher value indicates weaker softening) for low-alloy steel under multi-stage hot deformation conditions, associated with dynamic recrystallization (DRX) behavior. The two horizontal axes denote the natural logarithm of the Zener-Hollomon parameter of the first deformation stage ($\ln Z_p^{(I)}$) and second deformation stage ($\ln Z_p^{(II)}$), both ranging from 22 to 36, while the vertical axis represents the first-stage DRX fraction $\varphi_d^{(I)}$ ranging from 0 to 1, with four sliced surfaces at different $\varphi_d^{(I)}$ values for visualization. Visual observation confirms that $k_s$ decreases (softening effect enhances) with increasing difference between $\ln Z_p^{(I)}$ and $\ln Z_p^{(II)}$, and with rising $\varphi_d^{(I)}$, consistent with reported results. This figure quantifies the dependence of stress softening behavior on two-stage deformation parameters and intermediate DRX fraction, supporting the development of high-accuracy constitutive models for flow stress prediction under non-constant hot deformation conditions.

This figure presents experimental and 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulated initial mixed-grain microstructure of the studied low-alloy steel for automobile hub bearings. Subfigure (a) shows the optical metallographic observation result, with average grain sizes of 127.63 μm, 131.59 μm, 166.00 μm on three orthogonal sections respectively, the scale bar is 100 μm, and the characterized area on each section is 550 μm × 550 μm. Subfigure (b) displays the 3D CA simulated initial mixed-grain structure, with average grain sizes of 126.45 μm, 133.82 μm, 159.75 μm on three corresponding sections, and sliced layers along the X direction are also provided. The three grain size distribution histograms show: 1) experimental 2D grain size distribution: average grain size (AGS) = 141.66 μm, standard deviation (SD) = 54.54 μm, size range 10.22-265.20 μm; 2) CA simulated 2D grain size distribution: AGS = 140.01 μm, SD = 51.88 μm, size range 7.46-270.78 μm; 3) CA simulated 3D grain size distribution: AGS = 143.78 μm, SD = 58.25 μm, size range 6.45-283.29 μm. The high consistency between experimental and simulated grain size distributions validates that the developed 3D CA model can accurately reproduce the initial mixed-grain microstructure of the low-alloy steel, providing reliable initial microstructure input for subsequent dynamic recrystallization simulation under varying deformation conditions.

This figure is a flowchart of the 3D cellular automaton (CA) model based on inhomogeneous dislocation density, developed for simulating multi-stage dynamic recrystallization (DRX) of low-alloy steel under varying deformation conditions. The inputs include three categories: load conditions (temperatures, strain rates of two deformation stages, start strain of the second stage, end strain), initial microstructure (grain orientation, grain size, dislocation density, etc.), and model parameters (initial work hardening rate, saturation stress, DRX activation energy, plus constitutive calibrated parameters). The iterative simulation workflow first judges if total strain reaches the first-stage strain to adjust the stress softening coefficient and saturation stress, then calculates average and inhomogeneous dislocation density evolution. It further judges if dislocation density reaches the critical value to trigger inhomogeneous DRX nucleation, updates nucleation site parameters, calculates grain boundary migration, updates cell state variables, and stops when reaching the last step to output results including grain orientation distribution and dislocation density distribution. This model quantifies DRX behavior under varying thermomechanical conditions for hot processing microstructure control.

This set of four subplots (a-d) presents experimental (solid lines) and simulated (dashed lines with circles) dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction as a function of time for low-alloy steel under hot compression, at deformation temperatures 850°C (a), 950°C (b), 1050°C (c), and 1150°C (d) respectively, with four tested strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹, 0.1 s⁻¹, 1 s⁻¹, 10 s⁻¹. Clear trends are observed: higher strain rate leads to earlier onset and faster completion of DRX at the same temperature, while elevated temperature also significantly accelerates DRX kinetics. The simulated DRX evolution curves show good agreement with experimental measurements, which validates the accuracy of the developed inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton model for DRX behavior prediction under varied thermomechanical conditions.

This set of four subplots present experimental (solid lines) and simulated (dashed lines with circles) true stress-true strain curves of the studied low-alloy steel under hot compression deformation. Subplots (a), (b), (c), (d) correspond to deformation temperatures of 850℃, 950℃, 1050℃, 1150℃ respectively, each containing curves at four strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹, 0.1 s⁻¹, 1 s⁻¹, 10 s⁻¹. It is observed that peak stress increases with rising strain rate and decreases with increasing deformation temperature. The high consistency between experimental and simulated results validates the accuracy of the developed constitutive model and inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton model for flow stress prediction under different hot deformation conditions, supporting the models' applicability for dynamic recrystallization behavior simulation during hot processing.

This set of plots presents true stress-true strain curves of low-alloy steel during hot compression at 950 °C under transient strain rate conditions, comparing experimental flow stress data with simulation results from two constitutive models. Subplots (a) and (b) correspond to strain rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at pre-strain of 0.2 or 0.4, while (c) and (d) correspond to strain rate decrease from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at pre-strain of 0.2 or 0.4. Plots (a) and (c) use Model 1 for simulation, while (b) and (d) use Model 2. It is observed that Model 2, which incorporates the effect of softening coefficient kₛ on characteristic stresses, shows significantly better agreement with experimental results than Model 1: Model 1 exhibits obvious delay in predicting stress softening when strain rate drops, and fails to predict the lower steady-state stress after strain rate increase, while Model 2 accurately captures the flow stress evolution under both strain rate increase and decrease transient conditions, confirming its superior prediction performance for varying hot deformation conditions.

This set of 4 subplots presents experimental and simulated true stress-true strain curves of low-alloy steel during hot compression at a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ under varying temperature conditions. Subplots (a) and (b) correspond to deformation with initial temperature TI=1050 °C switched to TII=950 °C at pre-strain εI=0.2 or 0.4, simulated by Model 1 and Model 2 respectively. Subplots (c) and (d) correspond to deformation with initial temperature TI=950 °C switched to TII=1050 °C at pre-strain εI=0.2 or 0.4, simulated by Model 1 and Model 2 respectively. Each plot includes experimental flow stress curves, simulated curves from the corresponding model, and reference constant-temperature flow stress curves at 950 °C and 1050 °C. The comparison shows that Model 2 achieves significantly better agreement with experimental stress evolution, including stress jump after temperature change and dynamic recrystallization induced softening, than Model 1, validating the higher accuracy of the modified constitutive model for flow stress prediction under variable-temperature thermomechanical processing conditions.

This group of 4 subplots presents force (N) versus stroke (mm) curves comparing experimental hot compression results of low-alloy steel with finite element simulation predictions from two constitutive models under varying deformation conditions. Subplots (a) and (b) test conditions at 950°C, with transient strain rate switches between 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.1 s⁻¹ applied at pre-strain of 0.2 and 0.4 respectively. Subplots (c) and (d) test conditions at constant strain rate 0.01 s⁻¹, with transient temperature switches between 950°C and 1050°C applied at pre-strain of 0.2 and 0.4 respectively. Model 1 consistently overpredicts the force value in the second deformation stage after the condition change, while the optimized Model 2, which adjusts t₀.₅ and characteristic stress parameters, shows much better agreement with experimental force evolution across all conditions. The results demonstrate that Model 2 has superior accuracy for load prediction under variable hot working conditions, validating its reliability for industrial hot forging applications.

This figure presents experimental and simulated true stress-true strain curves for low-alloy steel subjected to hot compression at 850 °C under two strain rates (0.01 s⁻¹ and 1 s⁻¹), paired with 3D cellular automaton simulated dislocation density distribution maps at true strains of 0.45 and 0.9 for each condition. The blue solid lines represent experimental flow stress, while purple dotted lines represent simulation results, showing good agreement between model prediction and experiment. For the 0.01 s⁻¹ condition, dislocation density ranges from 1×10¹¹ m⁻² to 2.821×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε=0.45, rising to a maximum of 3.872×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε=0.9. For the higher 1 s⁻¹ strain rate, flow stress is significantly higher, with dislocation density ranging up to 1.087×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε=0.45 and 1.198×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε=0.9, as higher strain rate suppresses dynamic recrystallization nucleation and growth, leading to higher dislocation accumulation. This figure validates the accuracy of the developed inhomogeneous dislocation density 3D CA model for predicting hot deformation behavior and microstructure evolution of low-alloy steel.

This figure validates the accuracy of the developed inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton (CA) model for dynamic recrystallization (DRX) prediction of low-alloy steel, via comparing simulated and experimental results at 850 °C under two strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ and 1 s⁻¹, with hot compression up to true strain 0.9. Two line charts show the evolution of average grain size (3D CA simulated, decreasing with rising true strain) and DRX fraction (from both Eq.(45) analytical model and 3D CA simulation, increasing with rising true strain): at 0.01 s⁻¹, final DRX fraction reaches 84.98% and final simulated average grain size is 34.53 μm; at 1 s⁻¹, final DRX fraction is 49.59% and final simulated average grain size is 65.87 μm. Experimental EBSD inverse pole figure grain orientation maps, DRX fraction maps, and 2D cross-section simulated microstructures are also included: experimentally measured average grain size at 0.9 strain is 34.01±4.71 μm for 0.01 s⁻¹, 61.38±7.41 μm for 1 s⁻¹, which matches well with simulated results. Lower strain rate promotes higher DRX fraction and finer recrystallized grains, as longer deformation duration facilitates more DRX nucleation and growth.

This group of plots shows true stress (in MPa) versus true strain curves of low-alloy steel under transient varying hot deformation conditions, comparing experimental measurements and 3D Cellular Automaton (CA) simulation results. Subplot (a) presents strain rate jump from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.2 or 0.4 true strain under constant 950 ℃; (b) presents strain rate drop from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 0.2 or 0.4 true strain under constant 950 ℃; (c) presents temperature decrease from 1050 ℃ to 950 ℃ at 0.2 or 0.4 true strain under constant 0.01 s⁻¹ strain rate; (d) presents temperature increase from 950 ℃ to 1050 ℃ at 0.2 or 0.4 true strain under constant 0.01 s⁻¹ strain rate. The 3D CA simulated flow stress values show good agreement with experimental data, validating the accuracy of the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model for predicting flow stress evolution regulated by multi-stage dynamic recrystallization under variable deformation conditions.

This figure compares dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction evolution as a function of true strain for low-alloy steel, simulated by two models: Model 2 (phenomenological constitutive model) and the proposed inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D Cellular Automaton (3D CA) model, under four groups of varying thermomechanical deformation conditions, with constant-condition simulation results as baselines. Subplot (a) corresponds to 950 °C, strain rate switching from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at true strain 0.2 or 0.4; (b) corresponds to 950 °C, strain rate switching from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at true strain 0.2 or 0.4; (c) corresponds to 0.01 s⁻¹ strain rate, temperature decreasing from 1050 °C to 950 °C at true strain 0.2 or 0.4; (d) corresponds to 0.01 s⁻¹ strain rate, temperature increasing from 950 °C to 1050 °C at true strain 0.2 or 0.4. Key trends are observed: when Zener-Hollomon parameter (ZP) decreases transiently (subplots b, d), the 3D CA model predicts DRX fraction grows faster and eventually exceeds the constant-condition baseline, while Model 2 cannot exceed the baseline; when ZP increases transiently (subplots a, c), both models predict DRX fraction remains lower than the constant-condition baseline in the later deformation stage. The comparison demonstrates that the 3D CA model more accurately captures DRX evolution under varying deformation conditions, consistent with experimental flow stress observations.

This figure contains two line plot subplots of Model 1-simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction evolution versus true strain for low-alloy steel under transiently decreased Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_p) conditions. Subplot (a) tests 950°C constant temperature, comparing constant strain rate conditions (0.01 s⁻¹, 0.1 s⁻¹) with two-stage deformation: first stage at 0.1 s⁻¹ to intermediate true strain 0.2 or 0.4, then second stage at 0.01 s⁻¹ up to total strain 0.9. Subplot (b) tests 0.01 s⁻¹ constant strain rate, comparing constant temperature conditions (950°C, 1050°C) with two-stage deformation: first stage at 950°C to intermediate true strain 0.2 or 0.4, then temperature raised to 1050°C for the second deformation stage. DRX fraction after the transient condition change falls between the two constant-condition curves, with higher intermediate transition strain leading to DRX kinetics closer to the first-stage constant condition. This figure demonstrates the influence of transition strain on DRX evolution under varying thermomechanical processing conditions, as predicted by Model 1.

This set of 4 subplots compares experimental average grain size (AGS, unit: μm) and 3D cellular automaton (3D CA) simulation results of low-alloy steel under different transient deformation conditions, with true strain as the horizontal axis. Subplot (a) shows results at 950 °C with strain rate changing from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹, at transition strain εI of 0.2 or 0.4; (b) shows results at 950 °C with strain rate changing from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹, at εI of 0.2 or 0.4; (c) shows results at constant strain rate 0.01 s⁻¹ with temperature decreasing from 1050 °C to 950 °C, at εI of 0.2 or 0.4; (d) shows results at constant strain rate 0.01 s⁻¹ with temperature increasing from 950 °C to 1050 °C, at εI of 0.2 or 0.4. In all subplots, the variation trend of simulated AGS with increasing true strain agrees well with experimental data (dots with error bars), verifying the accuracy of the developed 3D CA model for predicting dynamic recrystallization grain evolution under varying deformation conditions. AGS generally decreases with rising true strain, while different transient deformation conditions (strain rate/temperature change direction, transition strain) affect the degree and trend of grain refinement.

This figure compares experimental and simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) microstructure evolution of low-alloy steel under constant and transiently increasing strain rates at 950℃. Subplot (a) shows EBSD-reconstructed grain orientation maps and corresponding grain orientation spread (GOS) maps at 0.9 strain for constant 0.1 s⁻¹ strain rate and transient strain rate increase at εI=0.2/0.4, with DRX fractions of 95.0%, 94.4%, 96.3%, and average grain sizes of 28.89±3.09μm, 31.93±2.03μm, 37.16±2.26μm respectively. Subplot (b) compares reconstructed microstructures at 0.4/0.5 strain for transient strain rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ and constant 0.1 s⁻¹, with average grain sizes ranging from 50.53±5.62μm to 65.33±5.30μm. Subplots (c-e) present 3D cellular automaton simulated microstructure evolution under different strain rate paths, showing consistent DRX fraction increase and grain size refinement trend with rising strain, matching experimental observations well to validate the 3D CA model accuracy for DRX prediction under varying deformation conditions.

This figure consists of two bar chart subplots comparing experimental and 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction of low-alloy steel under varying hot deformation conditions after reaching 0.4 true strain. Subplot (a) presents data for varying strain rate conditions, including both increasing and decreasing strain rate at 0.4 strain, while subplot (b) presents data for varying temperature conditions, including both decreasing and increasing temperature at 0.4 strain. The x-axis for both subplots is true strain (0.4, 0.5, 0.9), and y-axis is DRX fraction. Across all tested conditions, DRX fraction increases with rising true strain. The 3D CA simulation results show excellent agreement with experimental measurements, with minimal numerical deviations between the two datasets for all conditions. These results validate the accuracy of the developed inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model for predicting DRX evolution under transient strain rate and temperature variation deformation conditions.

This figure presents experimental and simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) microstructure evolution of low-alloy steel under constant and decreasing temperature deformation at 0.01 s⁻¹. Subfigure (a) shows EBSD reconstructed prior austenite microstructures and corresponding GOS maps at 0.9 strain for samples deformed at constant 950°C, and decreasing temperature from 1050°C to 950°C with εI=0.2 and 0.4, with DRX fractions of 97.2%, 96.1%, 97.2% and average grain sizes of 52.01±3.48μm, 53.29±3.77μm, 60.93±2.91μm respectively. Subfigure (b) compares reconstructed microstructures at 0.4 strain (1050°C deformation), 0.5 strain (after temperature drop) and 0.5 strain under constant 950°C, with average grain sizes of 80.79±5.43μm, 74.40±5.36μm, 56.97±5.24μm. Subfigures (c) and (d) show 3D cellular automaton simulated microstructure evolution under constant 950°C and decreasing temperature conditions respectively, with DRX fractions and average grain sizes marked for each strain level. White grains represent matrix, green grains are first-stage DRX grains formed at 1050°C_0.01s⁻¹, red grains are second-stage DRX grains formed at 950°C_0.01s⁻¹. Simulated results agree well with experimental EBSD observations, validating the accuracy of the 3D CA model for predicting multi-stage DRX behavior under varying temperature deformation.

This figure presents experimental and simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) microstructure evolution of low-alloy steel under hot compression at 950°C, where strain rate transiently decreases from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at ε=0.4. Subfigure (a) shows experimental EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps and grain orientation spread (GOS) maps characterizing grain orientation and DRX fraction: at 0.4 strain, DRX fraction is 38.9% with average grain size 81.50±6.32μm; at 0.5 strain, DRX fraction rises to 95.2% with average grain size 52.92±3.35μm; at 0.9 strain, DRX fraction reaches 97.7% with average grain size 50.71±3.29μm. Subfigure (b) shows 3D cellular automaton simulation results, with white as matrix, green as first-stage DRX grains, red as second-stage DRX grains. Simulated DRX fractions are 42.9%, 99.1%, 99.8% at 0.4, 0.5, 0.9 strain respectively, matching experimental trends well, validating the accuracy of the developed 3D CA model for multi-stage DRX prediction under varying deformation conditions.

This figure presents experimental and simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) microstructure evolution of low-alloy steel under constant and step-increased temperature hot compression at strain rate 0.01 s⁻¹. Subfigure (a) compares EBSD reconstructed microstructures and corresponding GOS maps at 0.5 strain, for constant 1050°C deformation (average grain size 80.01±4.30μm, DRX fraction 98.6%) and deformation with temperature increased from 950°C to 1050°C at ε₁=0.4 (average grain size 62.63±4.11μm, DRX fraction 97.3%). Subfigure (b) compares reconstructed microstructures at 0.9 strain for the two conditions, with average grain sizes 81.30±4.39μm and 79.69±4.78μm respectively. Subfigures (c) and (d) show 3D cellular automaton simulated microstructure evolution for constant 1050°C and temperature-increase conditions respectively, with DRX fractions and average grain sizes matching experimental results well, validating the accuracy of the developed 3D CA model for DRX prediction under varying deformation conditions.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton simulation results of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction and average dislocation density evolution in low-alloy steel under transiently increased Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_P) conditions. Subplots (a) and (b) show DRX fraction as a function of true strain: (a) corresponds to a strain rate jump from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 950°C, initiated at pre-strains ε_I of 0.2 or 0.4, while (b) corresponds to a temperature drop from 1050°C to 950°C at 0.01 s⁻¹, initiated at ε_I of 0.2 or 0.4. These DRX plots decompose total DRX into contributions from first-stage DRX grains (DRXGs_I, orange dashed line) and second-stage DRX grains (DRXGs_II, green dashed line). Subplots (c) and (d) show average dislocation density (in units of 10¹³ m⁻²) as a function of true strain, with the same deformation variation conditions as (a) and (b) respectively, separating contributions from matrix grains (purple solid line), DRXGs_I (orange dashed line), and DRXGs_II (green dashed line). Key observed trends include: total DRX fraction increases with true strain, DRXGs_II nucleation is delayed after Z_P elevation, DRXGs_I fraction decreases as DRXGs_II fraction rises, and DRXGs_II exhibit lower dislocation density than both matrix and DRXGs_I, providing the driving force for DRXGs_II to refine the matrix and DRXGs_I. These results confirm that the inhomogeneous dislocation density 3D CA model accurately captures multi-stage DRX behavior under varying thermomechanical deformation conditions.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton simulated microstructure and dislocation density distribution evolutions on YZ surface of low-alloy steel under transient Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_P) variation conditions, with 100 μm scale bars for all subgraphs. Subfigure (a) corresponds to strain rate jump from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.2 strain under 950°C, showing results at 0.4 and 0.6 strain; (b) corresponds to strain rate jump at 0.4 strain under 950°C, showing results at 0.6 and 0.8 strain; (c) corresponds to temperature drop from 1050°C to 950°C in strain range 0.4-0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹, showing results at 0.6 and 0.8 strain. White grains represent unrecrystallized matrix, green grains represent first-stage dynamic recrystallization (DRX) grains, red grains represent second-stage DRX grains; color scales quantify dislocation density ranging from ~1×10¹¹ m⁻² to ~2.6×10¹⁴ m⁻² for different conditions. Second-stage DRX grains are observed to preferentially nucleate at grain boundaries, with lower dislocation density than matrix and first-stage DRX grains, driving grain refinement and flow stress softening. This figure validates that dislocation density difference between multi-stage DRX grains is the core mechanism of stress softening under varying deformation conditions.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulated evolutions of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction and average dislocation density for low-alloy steel under transient Zener-Hollomon parameter (ZP) decreasing conditions. Subplots (a) and (c) correspond to test conditions where strain rate drops from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 950°C, with the deformation condition change initiated at true strain 0.2 or 0.4: (a) plots total DRX fraction plus separate contributions from first-stage (DRXGsI) and second-stage (DRXGsII) recrystallized grains, while (c) plots average dislocation density for matrix grains, DRXGsI and DRXGsII. Subplots (b) and (d) correspond to conditions where temperature increases from 950°C to 1050°C at 0.01 s⁻¹, with the change initiated at true strain 0.2 or 0.4, showing DRX fraction (b) and dislocation density (d) evolution respectively. Key observed trends include: DRXGsI fraction first rises rapidly after ZP decrease then declines as DRXGsII nucleate and grow; matrix and DRXGsI dislocation density drops rapidly in the second stage due to enhanced dynamic recovery and DRX grain refinement, leading to faster stress softening than constant deformation conditions. The figure validates that the developed CA model accurately captures multi-stage DRX behavior under varying hot forming conditions, supporting quantitative microstructure control for industrial hot forging of the studied low-alloy steel.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on YZ surface of low-alloy steel at 950 °C under two deformation conditions. Subfigure (a) shows results for transiently decreasing strain rate from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 0.4 strain: microstructure maps (white: matrix grains, green: first-stage dynamic recrystallization (DRX) grains, red: second-stage DRX grains) and corresponding dislocation density maps at 0.4, 0.42, 0.45 strain, with total DRX fractions of 42.9%, 85.8%, 96.7% respectively, and overall dislocation density decreasing as strain increases. Subfigure (b) shows results for constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ at 0.42 strain, with DRX fraction of 83.0%. It demonstrates that DRX fraction rises and average dislocation density reduces with increasing strain under transient decreasing strain rate, and DRX behavior differs between transient and constant strain rate conditions, validating the capability of the developed CA model to capture multi-stage DRX evolution under varying deformation conditions.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on YZ surface of low-alloy steel deformed at 0.01 s⁻¹ under different temperature conditions. Subfigure (a) corresponds to temperature increasing from 950 °C to 1050 °C at strains of 0.2, 0.23, 0.3: at 0.2 strain, total dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction is 3.8%, dislocation density ranges 1×10¹¹ to 1.529×10¹⁴ m⁻²; at 0.23 strain, first-stage DRX fraction is 39.0% and total DRX fraction 41.4%, with dislocation density ranging 1×10¹¹ to 1.240×10¹⁴ m⁻²; at 0.3 strain, first-stage DRX 57.7% and total DRX 98.1%, dislocation density ranges 1×10¹¹ to 2.984×10¹³ m⁻². White, green, red grains represent unrecrystallized matrix, first-stage DRX, second-stage DRX respectively. Subfigure (b) is constant 1050 °C at 0.23 strain, with 45.0% DRX fraction, dislocation density 1×10¹¹ to 3.067×10¹³ m⁻². Key trends show increasing strain under rising temperature elevates DRX fraction and reduces average dislocation density, verifying the model can capture multi-stage DRX evolution and inhomogeneous dislocation distribution under varying hot deformation conditions.

This group of four subplots (a-d) presents 3D cellular automaton simulated average dynamic recrystallization (DRX) grain size (unit: μm) evolution as a function of true strain (range: 0.2 to 0.9) for low-alloy steel under multi-stage varying thermomechanical deformation conditions, with results compared to constant deformation condition benchmarks. Subplot (a) evaluates transient strain rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at pre-strains of 0.2 or 0.4 at 950 °C; (b) evaluates temperature decrease from 1050 °C to 950 °C in strain ranges 0.2-0.3 or 0.4-0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹; (c) evaluates transient strain rate decrease from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at pre-strains 0.2/0.4 at 950 °C; (d) evaluates temperature increase from 950 °C to 1050 °C in strain ranges 0.2-0.3/0.4-0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹. Curves correspond to grain size under constant conditions (purple), first-stage DRX grains (DRXGsI, orange), and second-stage DRX grains (DRXGsII, green). Key observed trends: DRXGsI are larger than DRXGsII when the Zener-Hollomon parameter (ZP) increases transiently, while DRXGsI grow to match constant-condition grain sizes and DRXGsII nucleate without delay when ZP decreases, leading to minimal final grain size difference from constant conditions. The figure supports the conclusion that transient ZP increase yields larger final average grain sizes with higher pre-strain, while transient ZP decrease has negligible effect on final grain size, validating multi-stage DRX behavior predictions for quantitative hot forging microstructure control.
